topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification flashed across my screen, a sterile, unwelcome beacon in the quiet of my apartment. My stomach clenched, a cold knot tightening beneath my ribs. I clicked it open, and the words swam before my eyes: "Congratulations! Your piece, 'The Silent City,' has been published under the name of Elias Vance." A sharp, metallic taste flooded my mouth. I reread the email three times, my fingers trembling as they hovered over the keyboard. It felt like a physical blow, the air suddenly too thin to breathe. I stood up abruptly, pacing the length of my small living room, running a hand repeatedly through my hair until it felt slick with sweat. Every shadow in the room seemed to shift, mocking the sudden, sickening realization that someone else had claimed my voice, my words, my very creation. I sank onto the sofa, burying my face in my hands, the silence of the room amplifying the frantic drumming in my chest.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, the pixels blurring into meaningless shapes. The ink on the page, the carefully constructed arguments I had poured into those words—they were suddenly someone else's property. A deep, nauseating wave washed over me, making my vision swim. I pushed back from the desk so fast my chair scraped loudly against the hardwood floor. My breath hitched, shallow and quick. I walked to the window, pulling the curtain aside just enough to see the indifferent cityscape below. How could this happen? The anger was a slow, hot burn starting low in my belly, spreading outward until my jaw felt clenched tight enough to crack. I turned back, my knuckles white where I gripped the windowsill. I needed proof, a tangible thing to fight against this invisible theft, but all I had was this hollow, echoing sense of being utterly undermined.

[story 3]

The phone call had ended, leaving a ringing silence in its wake. I held the receiver tightly, the plastic digging into my palm. When I finally lowered it, the sound of my own ragged breathing filled the space. It wasn't just a mistake; it was a profound erasure. I walked into the kitchen, mechanically pouring a glass of water, the cold liquid doing nothing to quell the internal tremor. Each movement felt labored, heavy, as if moving through thick mud. I looked at my reflection in the darkened glass of the refrigerator—a stranger with wide, haunted eyes. A slow, deliberate shake of my head followed, a gesture of utter disbelief curdling into something sharp and bitter. I set the glass down with excessive force, the clink echoing unnervingly loud. The air in the room felt suffocating, heavy with the weight of this injustice.
